title: [Feature]: Kube Play under Systemd - Default restart policy should be never
body:
### Feature request description

One of the reasons for opening the issue for supporting Kubernetes Jobs: https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/17011, was a discussion about how the responsibilities to restart pods should be split between Podman and systemd when running `kube play` inside a systemd service. The idea was that since Jobs mandate that the `restartPolicy` be set to either `Never` or `OnFailure`, using them with parallelism and completions set to 1, podman will not restart the pod leaving the decision to systemd.

But, maybe a simpler solution would be to use a default value of `Never` for the `restartPolicy` when running under systemd (using the --service-cotainer flag). 

### Suggest potential solution

A clear and concise description of what you want to happen.

### Have you considered any alternatives?

A clear and concise description of any alternative solutions or features you've considered.

### Additional context

Add any other context or screenshots about the feature request here.

comments.body: After the last conversation, I felt that Kubernetes Jobs would do that.  Changing the restart policy is potentially a breaking change for some users.

Jobs will do that but mostly because they force the restartPolicy to not be Always.
As for breaking behavior, 4.4 already includes behavior changes when using --service-container, e.g. default log-driver

> As for breaking behavior, 4.4 already includes behavior changes when using --service-container, e.g. default log-driver

I consider that to be an improvement.  But changing the restart policy can be a big break for users relying on it.
I thought the agreement was to differentiate between a JOB(Restart=never) and a Deployment (Restart=Always).

I am also torn between keeping the status quo and changing the restart policy.  One (major) issue with the current systemd integration is that there is no way (yet?) to propagate the exit status to the service container.  Hence, even if all containers fail miserably and exit non-zero while the service container exits cleanly.

At the time of writing, I feel more comfortable leaving things as they are now but remain open to (new) use cases.  One thing to continue thinking about is definitely why/if/when we want to propagate exit codes to the service container.
Now I'm a bit confused. IIUC systemd will not get the containers' exit code in any case. So, using Jobs won't help us either, will they?

> Now I'm a bit confused. IIUC systemd will not get the containers' exit code in any case. So, using Jobs won't help us either, will they?

Not yet.  We discussed the exit-code propagation last week.  Job is the right way to have a one-shot-like experience but the exit-code propagation is still an open question.  We looked at how Kubernetes handles things and IIRC all containers must succeed and if one doesn't there is some exponential back-off with a counter limit.

Well if there is only one container, then we should propagate.
More then one container, and all fail, propagate.
One or more containers and all succeed, then propagate success.
The only question is what do to with at least one fails and at least one succeeds.
In OneShot, I believe this should be handled as success, We could spice it up by allowing an attribute that told podman to ignore failures for a particular container.

For sure the question is about the last case. But, I'm not sure I agree with you. In K8S, the pod state is deemed as Error if any of its containers returned an error. As a result, a Job run will be considered as Failed if one of the containers failed and once all retries are done, the job will have a status of failed.
I think this makes more sense since the orchestrator (be it K8S or podman) cannot differentiate between the containers.

> The only question is what do to with at least one fails and at least one succeeds.
In OneShot, I believe this should be handled as success, We could spice it up by allowing an attribute that told podman to ignore failures for a particular container.

I think we should do what K8s does.

> In K8S, the pod state is deemed as Error if any of its containers returned an error. As a result, a Job run will be considered as Failed if one of the containers failed and once all retries are done, the job will have a status of failed.
I think this makes more sense since the orchestrator (be it K8S or podman) cannot differentiate between the containers.

Exactly that's what we've been discussing/looking at last week :+1:

> Well if there is only one container, then we should propagate.

I am not sure we should unconditionally.  The propagation should be determined by the "Kind".

Let's first get propagation nailed down for Jobs, then we can consider opting-in for other kinds.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
